title: Chronos: Learning the Language of Time Series
authors: Abdul Fatir Ansari, Lorenzo Stella, Caner Turkmen, Xiyuan Zhang, Pedro Mercado, Huibin Shen, Oleksandr Shchur, Syama Sundar Rangapuram, Sebastian Pineda Arango, Shubham Kapoor, Jasper Zschiegner, Danielle C. Maddix, Hao Wang, Michael W. Mahoney, Kari Torkkola, Andrew Gordon Wilson, Michael Bohlke-Schneider, Yuyang Wang (AWS AI Labs / Amazon and academic affiliates)
venue: Transactions on Machine Learning Research (TMLR), 10/2024; arXiv:2403.07815v3
tokenization_method: Mean-scaling + uniform quantization of real values into a fixed discrete vocabulary, then standard LM training. Each scalar observation x_i is mapped to a token by (1) mean-scaling: divide by s = (1/C) * sum(|x_i|) over the context (m=0, so zeros are preserved); (2) quantization q: assign the scaled value to one of B bins with centers c_1<...<c_B over [c1=-15, cB=+15] and B-1 mid-point edges (uniform binning chosen over quantile binning because downstream test distributions differ from training). Vocabulary V_ts = {B bin tokens} + {PAD, EOS}; total |V_ts| = 4096 (so B = 4094 quantization bins). The only architectural change to the off-the-shelf LM is resizing input/output embedding layers to |V_ts|. Dequantization d(j)=c_j maps a predicted token back to its bin-center value, then inverse mean-scaling recovers the forecast.
feature_types_handled: numerical only (univariate real-valued time series; xi in R). No categorical, no static covariates, no explicit missing-value modeling beyond using PAD to replace missing entries. Time/frequency features (day-of-week, etc.) are deliberately ignored — the series is treated as a bare sequence.
token_granularity: per-value (one token per timestep value = one quantization bin); NOT per-feature and NOT a learned codebook — bins are fixed, uniform, data-independent.
numerical_strategy: quantile-binning considered but rejected; uniform fixed binning used: mean-scale by context mean-absolute-value, then assign to one of 4094 uniformly-spaced bins on [-15,+15]. Distance-unaware: cross-entropy treats bins as unordered classes (regression-via-classification), so 'nearness' of adjacent bins must be learned from data, not imposed.
high_cardinality_handling: n/a (no categorical features). Note: the vocabulary itself (4096) is a fixed, low-cardinality codebook over binned numeric values, which is the opposite problem from high-cardinality categoricals.
temporal_handling: the entire method is temporal: autoregressive next-token prediction over the value sequence, context length 512, prediction length 64, probabilistic multi-step forecast via autoregressive sampling of token IDs then dequantize+unscale. But it carries NO timestamp/frequency features — position is purely sequence order.
scales_to_many_features: Does NOT address many features — it is strictly univariate (single channel). The paper's Discussion explicitly flags covariates (time-varying + static) and multivariate forecasting as open future work, suggesting task-specific adapters or stacking with LightGBM rather than a native multi-feature tokenizer. Cost note: inference is autoregressive and slow; the authors did not scale beyond 710M precisely because of slow inference.
downstream_model: Off-the-shelf transformer LMs with no architectural modification: primarily encoder-decoder T5 (Mini 20M, Small 46M, Base 200M, Large 710M) plus a decoder-only GPT-2 base (90M) to show the recipe works for decoder-only too.
pretraining_objective: Standard categorical cross-entropy (next-token prediction) over the 4096-token vocabulary — i.e. regression-via-classification. No distance-aware or ordinal loss; arbitrary (incl. multimodal) output distributions are learnable. Trained 200K steps, AdamW (wd 0.01), lr 1e-3 linearly annealed to 0, effective batch 256, on 8xA100.
key_results: On 42 datasets (Benchmark I = 15 in-domain, Benchmark II = 27 zero-shot): (a) Chronos significantly outperforms statistical, task-specific deep, and other pretrained models in-domain; (b) zero-shot, Chronos comfortably beats statistical baselines and other pretrained models (Lag-Llama, Moirai-1.0-R, ForecastPFN, LLMTime) and matches the best task-specific deep models (PatchTST, TFT, DeepAR, N-HiTS) that were trained on each target dataset. Metrics: WQL (9 quantile levels) and MASE. Training corpus ~890K real univariate series (~84B observations) plus 10M TSMixup augmentations and 1M KernelSynth synthetic series, sampled 9:1.
baselines_compared: Statistical: Naive, SeasonalNaive, AutoETS, AutoARIMA, AutoTheta, SCUM ensemble. Deep task-specific: WaveNet, DeepAR, N-BEATS, TFT, DLinear, PatchTST, N-HiTS, GPT4TS. Pretrained: Lag-Llama, Moirai-1.0-R, ForecastPFN, LLMTime.
transferable_idea: Scale-then-uniformly-quantize a continuous value into a small fixed codebook (~4k bins over a normalized range) and train ANY off-the-shelf transformer on the resulting token IDs with plain cross-entropy — 'regression via classification' that needs zero architecture changes and yields a flexible (multimodal) predictive distribution for free. For 70+ heterogeneous features this is a candidate numeric-value tokenizer for the time-varying half: per-feature mean-scale + shared/ per-feature bin vocabulary, letting a transformer attend over a long sequence of value-tokens.
limitations: (1) Univariate only — no covariates, no static attributes, no multivariate cross-series modeling; covariate handling deferred to adapters or LightGBM stacking. (2) Fixed [-15,+15] range = overflow for sparse/spiky series (when 1 > 15/n the spike value is unrepresentable) and loss of precision for large-mean small-variance series (token spacing 30s/(B-1) collapses nearby values); mitigated only by inference-time renormalization heuristics. (3) Cannot extrapolate strong/exponential trends (range is bounded; training data lacks exponential trends). (4) Distance-unaware cross-entropy ignores the ordinal structure of bins. (5) Slow autoregressive inference caps model size and latency. (6) Ignores all time/frequency metadata.
pitfalls: B=4094 not 4096 (4096 = bins + PAD + EOS) — easy to misquote. Mean-scaling divisor is mean ABSOLUTE value of the CONTEXT only, not std and not global. The headline 'zero-shot beats specialized models' is on the SAME zero-shot benchmark Chronos's training corpus was curated to be diverse for; in-domain wins are unsurprising since those datasets were in training. Uniform (not quantile) binning is a deliberate generalization choice but means resolution is wasted on rarely-visited extreme bins. The 'language of time series' framing is marketing for what is mechanically value-binning + cross-entropy; no NLP semantics transfer.
relevance_to_topic: Directly anchors the time-series-foundation-models route: shows a value-tokenization (scale -> uniform bin -> fixed vocab -> cross-entropy) that turns continuous numeric features into discrete tokens for a vanilla transformer — reusable for the numeric/time-varying half of a 70+-feature set, but it offers NO mechanism for fusing many heterogeneous (categorical+static+multi-channel) features, which is the topic's hard part.
code_available: yes — https://github.com/amazon-science/chronos-forecasting (Apache-2.0, actively maintained, pretrained checkpoints on HuggingFace; datasets at huggingface.co/datasets/autogluon/chronos_datasets).
